当病毒式传播成为信息时：人工智能宣传的新时代

When Virality Is the Message: The New Age of AI Propaganda

time.com 作者 virgildotcodes 分数 60 评论 84 2026/04/06 22:16
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 欢迎来到人工智能驱动的宣传世界。 2026 年 3 月，人工智能生成的将美国总统唐纳德·特朗普和以色列总理本杰明·内塔尼亚胡描绘成乐高小人仔的视频开始充斥社交媒体。有些歌曲被设置为原创说唱曲目，其中的旋律令人惊讶，很可能也是人工智能生成的。他们讲述了战争的恐怖故事：废墟旁的小鞋子和塑料背包让人想起伊朗米纳布沙贾雷·塔耶贝女子学校的爆炸事件。一些人将这场战争与特朗普与被定罪的性犯罪者杰弗里·爱泼斯坦的关系联系起来。它们嘲讽地唤起了美国军队的惨败，乐高玩具士兵走进血河，或者装在挂着美国国旗的小棺材里回家。媒体报道将这些视频归咎于 Revayat-e Fath 研究所，其中一些视频除了在网上传播外，还在伊朗国家电视台播出。这个名字翻译过来就是“胜利的叙述”；报道将这些内容与伊斯兰革命卫队联系起来，该部队参与了其他影响行动和黑客活动。然而，大多数病毒视频都带有“爆炸新闻团队”的标志，这是一个自称为草根组织的组织，他们在 X 上声称自己是“伊朗乐高动画人”。 3月28日下午，他们抱怨自己的YouTube和Instagram账户被删除。在与《纽约客》的电子邮件通信中，他们声称是由学生经营且完全独立的；他们说，Revaat-e Fath 是他们视频的波斯语标题。 《爆炸新闻》到底是“爱国巨魔”在制作衷心内容来支持伊朗统治政权，还是利用雇佣军进行似是而非的推诿，还是一个假装独立的国营账户？已注销账户的社交媒体平台必须在诚信报告中证明其决定的合理性。其他视频更明显与国家有关：伊朗大使馆 X 的一个账户发布了一段人工智能动画，使用皮克斯电影《头脑特工队》(Inside Out) 的视觉效果来嘲笑特朗普。生成式人工智能使得大规模制作精美的宣传变得廉价且容易，也同样容易模糊官方信息和机会主义模仿之间的界限。将战争包装在娱乐的视觉语言中，使得冲突宣传更有可能传播，无论是谁制造的。社交媒体是一个开放的竞争环境：任何政府、代理团体或匿名帐户都可以争夺相同的受众，并且由于用户是积极的参与者，因此最引人注目的内容将赢得最大的影响力，无论其来源或意图如何。趋势是国际化的。白宫也一直在创作人工智能生成的内容。它发布了一段宣传“史诗愤怒行动”的视频，该视频以任天堂 Wii Sports 游戏系统的明显风格，用光标点击“开始”打开，然后在战争镜头（针对伊朗目标引爆的弹药）和卡通保龄球击球之间切换。在 White House X 帐户上看到的视频描绘了用战争镜头剪辑的不同视频游戏。白宫分享的 White House/XOther 视频将《使命召唤》中的场景与真实的空袭镜头、《侠盗猎车手：圣安地列斯》和《勇敢的心》的剪辑以及《壮志凌云》和《真人快打》的音频致敬。伊朗视频讲述了恐怖和特朗普受辱的故事；白宫的剪辑展现了统治地位和军事实力。但双方都用熟悉的娱乐语言打包战争。在一场真正的战争中，政府通过模因和玩具动画进行交流可能会让人感觉很奇怪。但这就是平台时代公开宣传的样子。其底层逻辑是这样的：借用游戏、迷因和儿童玩具的视觉语言；将它们放置在足够刺耳的环境中以吸引注意力；其余的事情就交给感兴趣的观众吧。社交媒体用户不需要认可消息即可传播它。他们只需要发现它具有足够的吸引力就可以观看和分享。十多年来，各国一直将社交媒体视为影响力的核心基础设施。他们实施全方位的宣传策略，同时通过多个渠道推送信息：国家电视台、官方账户、秘密网络和富有同情心的影响者，他们甚至可能不知道自己参与了该行动。结果不仅仅是广播。它将观众变成了一个比国家自己建立的任何渠道都要大的发行渠道。今天的宣传是参与性的；事物不仅仅会自行“病毒式传播”——人们也会与内容互动。这个模板可以说是在 2015 年设定的，当时伊斯兰国 (ISIS) 恐怖组织发布了一段四分钟的英语招募视频《No Respite》，该视频在巴黎巴塔克兰剧院袭击一周后首播。每一帧的设计都像一个沉浸在游戏文化中的西方青少年会感兴趣的东西：紧凑的剪辑，直接从动作电影预告片中走出来的节奏。都是为了嘲笑联军而制作的并让 ISIS 成为一个令人向往的品牌。当“群”感兴趣的用户分享时，平台就玩起了打地鼠游戏。研究人员开始推测模因战争的未来。 2020 年 4 月，中国新华社发布了《病毒往事》，乐高特有的宣传进入了官方媒体的节目范围，在这段视频中，乐高迷你兵马俑和戴着面具的医生与日益狂热、好战混乱的自由女神像就冠状病毒大流行进行了争吵。 “我们总是正确的，即使我们自相矛盾，”她一边说，一边连接着静脉注射器。该视频发布在 YouTube 和 Twitter 上（这两个网站在中国境内均被屏蔽，但它用于向海外发送消息），观看次数达数百万次。视频中存在明显的战略遗漏：中国医生乐高警告自由女神像病毒很糟糕，这对于我们这些在病原体开始传播时关注中国对医生的严厉监管的人来说似乎是无耻的讽刺。但这种格式以一种嘲讽的、孩子气的美学风格包装了一个简单的信息——“美国正在失败”，让人很容易观看，也可以轻松分享。这段视频绕过了官方媒体社论甚至真人演员可能引发的怀疑，同时戳中了美国疫情应对措施中真正的不一致之处。你不必喜欢中国共产党的世界观才能将其转发给朋友。你可以沮丧地同意其中的一点事实，或者仅仅因为它是超现实的：“你能相信这是中国政府做的吗？”俄罗斯也继承了乐高美学。在摩尔多瓦 2025 年议会选举之前，俄罗斯宣传人员传播了一些拼凑的乐高积木图片，上面描绘了悬挂乌克兰和摩尔多瓦国旗的士兵，旨在引发人们的担忧，即摩尔多瓦如果支持某些政党，就会被拖入战争。事实证明，塑料砖作为治国工具的用途非常广泛。将所有这些案例（ISIS 的动作预告片美学、中国、俄罗斯和伊朗的乐高积木、美国的视频游戏编辑）联系在一起的是对信息如何在网上传播的共同认识。社交媒体的货币不是权威或准确性，而是参与度。传播速度最快的内容结合了熟悉性和新颖性：人们立即就能认出的比喻，部署在足以让他们做出反应的不和谐的环境中。这就是为什么现在的宣传经常以模因、模仿或奇观的形式出现。用户不必同意它即可帮助分发它。他们只需要参与其中即可。嘲笑和讽刺在这种环境下特别流行，因为两者都很吸引人，而且很难反击。对乐高动画或 Wii 风格的轰炸模因的事实反驳几乎不可避免地显得乏味、无幽默感，而且语气不匹配。所有这一切的下游后果是，景观比现实接触到的人更多。白宫新闻秘书卡罗琳·莱维特吹嘘称，白宫的视频点击量已超过 20 亿次。一些分析人士评论称，伊朗的乐高视频已经超过了特朗普。无论谁赢得了病毒式传播竞赛，这两个数字都使任何有关实际事件的个别新闻报道的影响力相形见绌。被轰炸的学校、军事伤亡和燃烧的油田的故事都是通过模因处理的。人们越来越多地首先将战争视为内容，然后才将其视为新闻。正如宣传理论家雅克·埃鲁尔 (Jacques Ellul) 在 20 世纪 60 年代初所指出的那样，宣传随着承载它的通信系统而发展。在由算法、病毒式传播和现在的生成式人工智能塑造的社交媒体环境中，宣传越来越多地不再采用教义的形式，甚至也不再采用优化说服的消息传递的形式，而是作为传播的内容。只要观众有兴趣参与，国营账户就可以生成源源不断的乐高动画，甚至是深度伪造的战斗镜头。模仿账户——一些与国家有关，另一些只是为了追逐收入或影响力——可能会在该地区充斥着各种变体。这模糊了归因并使审核变得复杂，迫使平台对爆炸新闻等帐户做出困难且越来越不透明的判断。什么算国家宣传，什么算协调操纵，什么是有界限的？当提起宣传话题时，最常出现的问题与说服有关：这些视频真的能改变人们的想法吗？有时他们可能会。伊朗的乐高视频显然是为了破坏对战争的支持。但这个衡量成功的标准太狭隘了。更深层次的影响是环境方面的。病毒式宣传创造了一种氛围，通过这种氛围可以感知冲突：它塑造了什么是突出的，什么是荒谬的，什么是胜利者，什么是正义的。白宫视频并不是要改变反对者的态度，而是要改变他们的态度。他们正在为已经支持战争的观众表演主导地位（尽管超过一半的美国公众不这么认为）。但当迷因成为主要文本而新闻本身仍处于背景中时，奇观不必改变你的想法。它只需赢得目标受众注意力的战争即可。

Original Summary

  1. Welcome to the world of AI-driven propaganda. In March 2026, AI-generated videos depicting American President Donald Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu as LEGO minifigures began flooding social media. Some were set to original rap tracks with surprisingly catchy hooks, likely also AI-generated. They told stories of the horrors of war: little shoes and a plastic backpack near rubble evoked the bombing of the Shajareh Tayyebeh girls' school in Minab, Iran. Some connected the war to Trump’s ties to the convicted sex offender, Jeffrey Epstein. They mockingly evoked the brutal defeat of American forces, with LEGO toy soldiers walking into rivers of blood, or returning home in tiny caskets draped in American flags. Media coverage attributed the videos, some of which were broadcast on Iranian state television in addition to spreading online, to the Revayat-e Fath Institute. The name translates to “The Narration of Victory"; coverage linked the content to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, which has been implicated in other influence operations and hacking campaigns. However, most of the viral videos bear the logo of the “Explosive News Team,” a self-described grassroots group who have claimed credit on X for being “that Iranian Lego animation guys.” On the afternoon of March 28, they complained that their YouTube and Instagram accounts had been taken down. In email correspondence with The New Yorker, they claim to be student-run and totally independent; Revayat-e Fath, they say, is the Persian title of their videos. Are Explosive News “patriotic trolls” producing heartfelt content to support Iran’s ruling regime, paid mercenaries leveraged for plausible deniability, or a state-run account posturing as independent? Social media platforms that have taken their accounts down will have to justify their decisions in integrity reports. Other videos are more obviously state-linked: an Iranian Embassy X account posted an AI animation mocking Trump using visuals styled after the Pixar movie “Inside Out.” Generative AI has made it cheap and easy to produce polished propaganda at scale, and just as easy to blur the line between official messaging and opportunistic imitation. Packaging war in the visual language of entertainment makes conflict propaganda more likely to spread, regardless of who made it. Social media is an open playing field: any government, proxy group, or anonymous account can compete for the same audience, and because users are active participants, the most compelling content wins the most reach regardless of its origin or intent. The trend is international. The White House has been creating AI-generated content, too. It posted a video promoting Operation Epic Fury that opened with a cursor clicking “Start” in the unmistakable style of Nintendo’s Wii Sports gaming system, then cut between war footage (ordnance detonating against Iranian targets) and cartoon bowling strikes. Videos seen on the White House X account depicting different video games cut with war footage. The White House/XOther videos shared by the White House spliced scenes from Call of Duty with real airstrike footage, clips from Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas and Braveheart, and audio nods to Top Gun and Mortal Kombat. The Iranian videos tell stories of horror and of Trump’s humiliation; the White House clips project dominance and military might. But both package war in the familiar language of entertainment. Governments communicating through memes and toy animations during an actual shooting war may feel like a bizarre aberration. But this is what overt propaganda looks like in a platform age. The underlying logic works like this: borrow the visual language of games, memes, and children’s toys; place them in contexts jarring enough to capture attention; and let engaged audiences do the rest. Social media users don’t need to endorse a message to spread it. They only need to find it compelling enough to watch and share.For more than a decade, states have treated social media as core infrastructure for influence. They run full-spectrum propaganda strategies that push messaging through multiple channels simultaneously: state television, official accounts, covert troll networks, and sympathetic influencers who may not even know they’re part of the operation. The result is not just a broadcast. It turns the audience into a distribution channel larger than any the state could build on its own. Propaganda today is participatory; things don’t just “go viral” on their own — people engage with content. The template was arguably set in 2015, when the Islamic State (ISIS) terrorist group released No Respite, a four-minute English language recruitment video that premiered about a week after the Bataclan attacks in Paris. Every frame was built to look like something a Western teenager steeped in gaming culture would find appealing: tight cuts, pacing straight out of an action-movie trailer. It was made both to mock the coalition forces and to make ISIS feel like an aspirational brand. Platforms played whack-a-mole as “swarms” of intrigued users shared it. Researchers began to speculate about the future of memetic warfare. LEGO-specific propaganda entered the state-media repertoire universe in April 2020 when Chinese’s Xinhua News Agency released “Once Upon a Virus,” a video in which LEGO minifig terra cotta warriors and doctors in masks sparred with an increasingly feverish, belligerently chaotic Statue of Liberty about the coronavirus pandemic. “We are always correct, even though we contradict ourselves,” she says, hooked up to an IV. Posted on YouTube and Twitter—both of which are blocked within China, but which it uses for overseas messaging—it was viewed millions of times. There were glaring strategic omissions in the video: Chinese doctor Legos warning the Statue of Liberty that the virus was bad seemed brazenly ironic to those of us who followed China’s aggressive policing of doctors as the pathogen began to spread. But the format wrapped a simple message—“the U.S. is failing”—in a mockingly funny, childlike aesthetic that made it easy to watch, and frictionless to share. The video bypassed the skepticism that a state media editorial, or even just real human actors, would have triggered, while poking at real incoherencies in America’s pandemic response. You didn’t have to like the Chinese Communist Party’s worldview to forward it to a friend. You could pass it along in frustrated agreement with a grain of truth, or simply because it was surreal: “Can you believe the Chinese government made this?” Russia picked up the LEGO aesthetic as well. Ahead of Moldova’s 2025 parliamentary elections, Russian propagandists circulated images of fabricated LEGO sets depicting soldiers with Ukrainian and Moldovan flags, designed to stoke fears that Moldova would be dragged into the war if it supported certain parties. The plastic brick, it turns out, is remarkably versatile as an instrument of statecraft. What connects all of these cases—ISIS’ action-trailer aesthetic, China, Russia, and Iran’s LEGOs, the U.S.’ video game edits—is a shared recognition of how information moves online. The currency of social media is not authority or accuracy, it is engagement. The content that travels fastest combines familiarity with novelty: a trope people instantly recognize, deployed in a setting jarring enough to make them react. That is why propaganda now so often arrives as a meme, parody, or spectacle. Users do not have to agree with it to help distribute it. They only have to engage with it.Ridicule and satire are especially popular in this environment because both are captivating, and very difficult to counter. A factual rebuttal to a LEGO animation or Wii-style bombing meme almost inevitably looks plodding, humorless, and tonally mismatched. The downstream consequence of all this is that the spectacle reaches more people than the reality. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt bragged that the White House's videos had generated more than 2 billion impressions. Some analyst commentary argued that Iran’s LEGO videos had outpaced Trump’s. Regardless of who is winning the virality contest, both figures dwarf the reach of any individual news report about the actual events in question. Stories of a bombed school, military casualties, and burning oil fields are processed through memes. Increasingly, people encounter war first as content, and only later, if at all, as news. As propaganda theorist Jacques Ellul argued in the early 1960s, propaganda evolves with the communication systems that carry it. In a social media environment shaped by algorithms, virality, and now generative AI, propaganda increasingly takes the form not of doctrine, nor even of messaging optimized to persuade, but as content made to travel. State-run accounts can generate an endless stream of Lego animation, or even deepfaked battle footage, for as long as audiences appear interested in engaging with it. Copycat accounts—some state-linked, others simply chasing revenue or clout—can flood the zone with variants. That blurs attribution and complicates moderation, forcing platforms to make difficult and increasingly opaque judgments about accounts like Explosive News. What counts as state propaganda, what counts as coordinated manipulation, and what remains in bounds?When the topic of propaganda is raised, the question that most often follows relates to persuasion: Do these videos actually change minds? Sometimes they may. The Iranian LEGO videos are clearly designed to undermine support for the war. But that is too narrow a measure of success. The deeper effect is environmental. Viral propaganda creates the atmosphere through which a conflict is perceived: it shapes what feels salient, what seems ridiculous, who seems triumphant, what feels righteous. The White House videos aren’t trying to convert opponents; they’re performing dominance for an audience that already supports the war (even as more than half of the American public does not). But when the meme becomes the primary text and the news itself remains in the background, the spectacle doesn’t have to change your mind. It just has to win the war for the attention of your target audience.

评论（默认中文）

Alifatisk 2026/04/06 22:24
看到白宫推特账户发布的内容很奇怪，而且有点可怕。这是一个政府实体，一个超级大国，向世界发布极端和不严肃的内容。实在是太可笑了，我简直无法理解。我不明白其他国家的领导人怎么能认真对待美国现任政府。

从外部观察美国，我开始怀疑他们距离社会崩溃还有多远。过去十年里，事情似乎变得如此极端。或许现实并非如此，只是互联网吸纳的内容引起了反应。

Seeing what White House Twitter account is posting is bizarre, and a bit scary. This is a government entity, a superpower, posting extreme and unserious content to the world. It's so ridiculous that I can't barely comprehend it. I don't understand how leaders in other countries can take the current US administration seriously.

Looking at the US from outside, I am starting to wonder how close they are to a societal collapse. Things seem to have gotten so extreme over there the last decade. Or maybe its not like that in reality, and its just the internet siphoning content that gets reactions.

在 HN 中查看
keiferski 2026/04/07 00:29
虽然我认为美国社会肯定存在问题，但“美国社会接近崩溃”的想法并不比任何其他网络宣传观点更好，事实上，这是外国国家行为者的常见说法。

更好地思考这种无意义的在线内容：这只是在现代民主政治舞台上已被证明能够获胜的形式。不幸的是，成为一名严肃的专业人士不再与选民产生联系。发布大量愚蠢的模因似乎，或者至少几年前是这样——在我看来，当前政客使用的媒体策略在文化上已经过时了几年。

While I think American society definitely has problems, the idea that it's close to collapse is no better than any other online propaganda opinion, and in fact it's a common refrain pushed by foreign state actors.

A better way to think of this nonsensical online content: it's just the form that has been shown to win in the modern democratic political arena. Unfortunately, being a serious professional doesn't connect with voters anymore. Posting lots of goofy memes seems to, or at least it did a few years ago – IMO the media tactics used by current politicians are a few years out of date, culturally.

在 HN 中查看
danaris 2026/04/07 01:40
> 其接近崩溃的想法并不比任何其他网络宣传观点更好

不仅如此：你甚至如何 定义 “美国社会的崩溃”？

人们到底认为那会是什么样子？

清洗？

完全无政府状态？街头骚乱？

街上燃烧的金属垃圾桶的经典形象？

在某种程度上，像美国这样的现代社会 能 “崩溃”，这将是一个非常非常缓慢且不平衡的事情。最有可能的是这个国家的巴尔干化——在几年的时间里要么是事实上的分裂，要么是国家集团完全合法（或非法）的分裂。

> the idea that it's close to collapse is no better than any other online propaganda opinion

Not just that: how do you even define "the collapse of American society"?

What, exactly, do people think that would look like?

The Purge?

Complete anarchy? Riots in the streets?

The classic image of a burning metal garbage can in the street?

To the extent that a modern society like that of the US can "collapse", it's going to be a very, very slow and uneven thing. Most likely what it would look like is a Balkanization of the country—either de-facto, or full legal (or illegal) secession of groups of states, over the course of a number of years.

specproc 2026/04/07 02:06
我认为可能的情况是特朗普在中期后开始挖掘。考虑到他和他的船员周围漂浮着大量明目张胆的非法物品，这种情况很有可能发生。

然后有两条路：他要么成功，形成你在俄罗斯等地看到的那种“有管理的民主”。

或者他不成功，我们看看会发生什么。 ICE 是隶属于该政权的民兵组织。可能会变得辣。

从宪法上讲，我认为应该检查行政权力的框架已经被撕碎，或者至少揭示了它一直以来的样子：几乎是规范。

祝你好运。

I think the likely scenario is Trump digging in post-mid terms. This is very likely, given the amount of flagrantly illegal stuff he's got floating around him and his crew.

Then two paths: he's either successful, forming the sort of "managed democracy" you see in Russia etc.

Or he's unsuccessful, and we see what happens. ICE are a militia beholden to the regime. Could get spicy.

Constitutionally, I think the framework that's supposed to check executive power is already shredded, or at least revealed for what it's been all along: pretty much norms.

Good luck.

在 HN 中查看
tsumnia 2026/04/06 22:37
> 从外部看美国，我开始怀疑他们离社会崩溃还有多远

我们很好，关键是要记住离开互联网并记住现实与互联网不同。将互联网视为电视上的精彩片段频道 - 如果您不喜欢当前的“算法”，请更改“频道”。另外，请记住为什么科技总是推动广告拦截器 - 然后过滤掉需要您注意的事情。一旦你意识到很多新闻机构（政治、金融、科技等）都在使用与广告相同的深色图案，你就开始将它们从你的注意力中过滤掉。

我现在很喜欢在 Amazon Prime 上重看《邪恶力量》。

> Looking at the US from outside, I am starting to wonder how close they are to a societal collapse

We're fine, the trick is to remember to GET OFF THE INTERNET and remember that reality isn't the same as the Internet. Treat the Internet like a highlight reel channel on TV - if you don't like your current 'algorithm', then change 'channels'. Also, remember why tech has always pushed for Adblockers - then filter out the things demanding your attention. Once you realize a lot of news agencies (political, financial, tech, etc) is using the same dark patterns as ads, you start to filter them out of your attention.

I'm enjoying rewatching Supernatural on Amazon Prime right now.

在 HN 中查看
dragontamer 2026/04/06 22:48
哦当然。只要你不看战争，战争就不会发生。事实上，从技术上讲这不是一场战争，所以我们不应该关心它。

你是对的，我们必须更好地选择新闻来源。但答案并不是让自己沉浸在 Amazon Prime 上的愉快小说中或忽视时事。

答案是选择非点击诱饵/非末日滚动的新闻来源，提供更多可操作的新闻和更强的分析。为此，我选择了《大西洋月刊》，每周一次的杂志速度足够快，并为作者提供了足够的时间对时事进行深入而透彻的分析。

Twitter 和 Facebook 等快速传播的点击诱饵媒体就是垃圾。它通常是不正确的，充满了宣传，而被卷入其中的人看起来像白痴（与他们争论会降低你的智力）。找到更好的媒体，找到更好的人，并留下垃圾。

---------

选择您的新闻来源。否则，新闻来源就会选择你。自黄色新闻的早期以来，这一直都是事实。今天的媒体格局更难弄清楚，但今天仍然有写得很好的独立媒体，只要你出去支持他们并伸出援手。

Oh sure. The war isn't happening as long as you don't look at it. In fact, it's not technically a war so we shouldn't care about it.

You are correct in that we must be better about selecting our news sources. But the answer is not about drowning yourself in pleasant fiction on Amazon Prime or ignoring current events.

The answer is to pick non-clickbait / non-doomscrolling news sources that provide more actionable news and stronger analysis. I've picked The Atlantic for this, once a week magazine is fast enough and gives enough time for the writers to provide deep and through analysis on current events.

The fast moving clickbait media of Twitter and Facebook is trash. It's often incorrect, it's full of propaganda, and the people drawn into it seem like idiots (and arguing with them pulls your intelligence down). Find better media, find better people and leave the trash behind.

---------

Pick your news sources. Otherwise, the news sources will pick you. That's always been true since the early days of Yellow Journalism. The media landscape is harder to figure out today, but there continues to be well written independent media today, if only you went out to support them and reach out.

qsera 2026/04/07 00:27
>忽略当前事件

当然，意识到这一点很重要，但如果永远意识到当前的事件会让一个人对未来感到焦虑、无助和恐惧，那么我认为淹没在令人愉快的小说中比阅读新闻更好。

除了媒体想要你永久的关注、眼球和点击之外，仅仅对你的家感到焦虑和关心对任何事业都没有帮助。

>ignoring current events

Sure it is important to be aware, but If being perpetually aware of the current events makes one feel anxious, helpless and fearful of the future then I think it is better to drown in pleasant fiction than read news.

Just being anxious and concerned in your home has not helped any cause except of that of the media that want your perpetual attention, eye balls and clicks.

由于美国发动了一场可疑的战争并且在投下炸弹之前没有进行足够的尽职调查，伊朗数百或数千个家庭的生活被永久摧毁。我们能够忽视时事的唯一原因是，无论我们的政府在其他地方造成了多大的破坏，它们很少会得到应有的回报。
100s or 1000s of families' lives are permanently shattered in Iran because the US started a questionable war and didn't do enough due diligence before dropping bombs. dragonwriter 2026/04/07 07:34
> 当然，意识到这一点很重要，但如果永远意识到当前的事件会让一个人对未来感到焦虑、无助和恐惧，那么我认为淹没在愉快的小说中比阅读新闻更好。

上游声明之间存在差异 没有重大的实际问题，并且给人的印象是互联网造成了一种幻觉，应该断开连接以避免被误导 和你的说法 远离有压力的新闻来源可能对你的心理健康更好，无论这些新闻来源是否准确地描述了世界的真实状况。

你所说的可能大体上是正确的，但它与你所回应的论点是正交的。

> Sure it is important to be aware, but If being perpetually aware of the current events makes one feel anxious, helpless and fearful of the future then I think it is better to drown in pleasant fiction than read news.

There is a difference between the upthread claim that there is no significant real problem and the impression that there is is an illusion created by the internet which one should disconnect from to avoid being misled and your claim that it can be better for your mental health to cutoff from stressful news sources independently of whether those news sources accurately depict the real state of the world.

What you are saying may be broadly true, but it is orthogonal to the argument you were responding to.

在 HN 中查看
dragontamer 2026/04/07 03:26
好的写作不会让你感到焦虑。

这只是 Twitter/Facebook 的厄运滚动和糟糕的写作风格。他们正在向广告商出售点击量，而没有什么比厄运更能出售点击量了。

是的。停止使用互联网算法提要。不过，不要忽视这个消息。只需选择更好、更少焦虑和更少点击率的新闻即可。您认为 20 世纪 60 年代的民权抗议活动是由令人焦虑的标题党文章引发的吗？还是他们充满了无知的傻瓜，忽视了这个消息？

越南战争也是如此。你知道，那是一场真正的选秀，而且情况比我们现在伊朗的情况还要糟糕得多。你必须随时了解事件动态，但这并不意味着你必须接受这些标题诱饵磁铁的糟糕、引发焦虑的写作风格。

不。当时，他们选择了更好的新闻来源来激发行动并提供计划。今天也需要同样的条件。随时了解情况，但忽略垃圾、标题诱饵和白痴。

Good writing will not make you feel anxious.

That's just Twitter/Facebook doom scrolling and shitty writing style. They are selling clicks to advertisers and nothing sells clicks better than doom.

Yes. Stop going to internet algorithmic feeds. However, do not ignore the news. Simply choose better, less angst and less clickbaity news. Do you think the Civil Rights protests of the 1960s were informed by angsty clickbait articles? Or were they filled with ignorant dufuses who ignored the news?

Ditto with the Vietnam war. You know, the one with an actual draft and far worse situation than we have right now in Iran. You have to stay informed of events, but that doesn't mean you have to accept the shitty, angst inducing writing style of these clickbait magnets.

No. Back then, they chose better sources of news that inspired action and provided plans. Same is required for today. Stay informed, but ignore the crap, clickbait and idiots.

hsuduebc2 2026/04/07 01:47
我赞同这一点。消耗所有其他点击诱饵标题和某人的另一个无用分析只是让人神经紧张。媒体因你的关注而蓬勃发展，但你却不然。

我并不是说要忽略一切，只是不断地在循环中不会给你带来任何好处。你的焦虑是别人的商业模式。我不使用 X、看电视或类似媒体。不知道某个政客为了惹你生气而做了什么极其愚蠢的事，这绝对是可以的。

我时常在 YouTube 上观看一些独立分析师的节目，我不会错过任何重要的事情。真的。这是您现在可以为自己的心理健康做的最好、最简单的事情。

I second that. Consume every other click bait title and another useless analysis from someone is just nerve wrecking. Media thrive from your attention, but you do not.

I don't say to ignore everything bur being constantly in the loop gives you nothing. Your anxiety is someone else business model. I do not use media like X, watch tv or similiar. It's absolutely ok to not know what uterrly stupid some politician did with intent to get you mad.

I watch few independent analysts on youtube from time to time and I do not miss anything important. Really. It's the best and easiest thing you can do for your mental health now.

> 大西洋

我打开这个网站，我首先看到的是，

>这不是总统通常的沟通方式。 （关于特朗普）

让我尖叫的是，这是一个有偏见的网站。

所以我认为外行人不可能知道真相。甚至还差得远。你看过电影《摇狗》吗？这是一部1997年的电影。从那以后事情变得更糟了..

既然如此，还有什么意义呢？要么把你的生命置于危险之中，去这些地方了解真相并采取一些行动，要么只是走开，享受你现在拥有的一点点脆弱的平静。

> The Atlantic

I open this site and the first thing I see is,

>This Is Not How Presidents Typically Communicate. (About trump)

And it screams to me that this is a biased site.

So I think it is not possible for a layman to know the ground truth. Not even close. Have you seen the movie "Wag the dog?" It is a 1997 movie. Things have only gotten real worse since..

So given that, what is the point? Either put your life on line and go to these places and understand the truth and do something about it, or just zone out and enjoy the what ever little fragile peace that you have right now.

dragontamer 2026/04/07 09:53

我认为指出美国总统通常不会在复活节周日发出“赞美真主”的信息是相当事实的。像这样的亵渎行为肯定是新闻，值得登上《大西洋月刊》的头条新闻。

或者你认为“赞美真主”的信息对于现任美国总统来说是很正常的事情吗？这是一个简单的情况，没有理由在这个新闻上拐弯抹角。

Strange, are you familiar with the story or did you miss the news this weekend?

I think it's pretty factual to point out that US Presidents don't normally send the message "Praise Allah" on Easter Sunday. A blasphemy like that is certainly news and warrants the Atlantic's headline.

Or do you consider the message of "Praise Allah" to be a normal thing for sitting US Presidents to say? It's a straightforward situation, there's no reason to beat around the bush on this news item.

qsera 2026/04/07 10:44

他做得好吗？我认为只有时间才能证明一切，不可能通过对他所做或所说的每一件小事进行微观管理来回答这个问题。

这样做的媒体并没有真正做好向人民提供信息的工作。

所以我认为这个标题很适合这篇文章的标题“当病毒传播就是信息”。

I don't give a damn as long as he does his job. So should the people, and so should the media, if they are legit.

Is he doing a good job? I think only time can tell and it is impossible to answer that question by micromanaging every little thing he does or says.

A media that does that is not really doing a good job of informing the people.

So I think that headline fits the title of this post "When Virality Is the Message".

指出这不正常真的是违背事实的吗？你一开始就说《大西洋月刊》的标题不正确。

问题是根据你不同意的标题，什么是正常的，什么是不正常的。

我确实相信，一位现任美国总统发推文这样的事情是非同寻常的。确实值得对我们如何走到这一步进行一些分析。不像点击诱饵，我们注定要失败，而只是提醒我们作为一个国家已经走了多远。

dragontamer 2026/04/07 13:21
> 重点是这并不重要。

这是一场关于当前正在进行的冲突的积极谈判。人们简直要为这些话而死了。

不管你怎么说，但总统的话_确实_很重要。尤其是在战争、谈判和全球姿态的时期。

----------

成百上千的白痴为此而喘不过气来并不重要。但我可以说的是，在美国与伊朗的冲突中，这些话绝对重要。我们可以忽略那些无关紧要的专家。但这里讨论的是总统的话。

我碰巧喜欢《大西洋月刊》对这一特殊情况的整体分析。但你不能让我相信……这是“毫无意义的”。这真的是生死攸关的事，伙计。

------------

> 我不想再花时间在这上面了。

我们谁都不想在这上面花时间。但了解这场战争和冲突的进展情况非常重要。因为你不在乎而把头埋进沙子里……好吧……你可以选择以任何方式来完成这句话。

我们被迫花时间在这上面，因为它影响商业，影响军事准备。它影响到我们的全球定位、我们士兵的生命、伊朗人的生命等等。即使是无关的欧洲人（他们什么也没做）也因为这些事件引发的石油危机而被迫关注。

> The point is it does not matter.

Its an active negotiation going on about a currently on going conflict. People are literally dying over these words.

Say what you will about it, but the President's words _DO_ matter. Especially in times of war, negotiation, and global posturing.

----------

The hundreds of idiots hyperventilating about this don't matter. But what I can say is that in the USA vs Iran conflict, these words absolutely matter. We can ignore the pundits who don't matter. But the words being discussed here are the President's words.

And I happen to like The Atlantic's overall analysis on this particular situation. But you can't convince me that... this is "pointless". Its literally life or death here man.

-----------

> I don't want to spend any more time on this.

NONE OF US want to spend any time on this. But staying informed about how this war and conflict plays out is hugely important. Digging your head into the sand because you don't care is... well... take your pick at any way to finish that sentence.

We've been forced to spend time on it because it affects business, it affects military readiness. It affects our global positioning, the lives of our soldiers, the lives of Iranians, etc. etc. Even the unrelated Europeans (who did nothing) are forced to pay attention because of the oil crisis that flows out from these events.

在 HN 中查看
tsumnia 2026/04/07 06:53
[回复所有评论，所以如果我错过这里的上下文，您可能需要再次阅读该线程]

> 只要你不看战争，战争就不会发生。

我并非对战争一无所知，是的，我关注着它；然而我的心态源于几个月来不断发生的坏消息 某处。只有当地方层面有事情需要担心时，我才能对《世界》给予如此多的关注。所以我不会在互联网上从事“一切”。我亲自进行了这些对话。

> 但答案并不是让自己沉浸在 Amazon Prime 上的愉快小说中或忽视时事。

我不同意，但也没有溺水。而是以新冠疫情之前的方式进行在线互动。你知道，就像《行尸走肉》中的“我如何在僵尸末日中生存”或《权力的游戏》中的“下一个死者是谁”。被称为“评论部分”的 24/7 互联网聊天室只是想将话题引回到政治上。我也不同意我们处于“白痴”的观点，但我仍然喜欢这部电影。

> Twitter 和 Facebook 的快速传播的点击诱饵媒体是垃圾。它常常是不正确的，充满了宣传

每个聚合网站也是如此。议程设置理论 [1] 规定了您在任何一天收到的信息，并且具有自我推销的较小规模的 Discords 知道如何游戏化趋势算法（因为大多数都依赖于某种程度的基于发布后时间的“速度”）。再加上我们如何通过 A/B 测试缩略图 [2] 过度设计注意力，以及大脑如何读取这样的文本 [3]（这也针对电子邮件营销活动点击率进行了 A/B 测试），我很好奇“什么标题文本在情感上锚定了一种情绪”？

哎呀，离开互联网甚至没有大喊大叫[4]，这只是我滥用所有大写字母在大脑中的作用。

[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agenda-setting_theory

[2] https://netflixtechblog.com/selecting-the-best-artwork-for-v...

[3] https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10....

[4] 红迪版： https://v.redd.it/hlo2z6a6rctg1，推特版本： https://x.com/amgaweda/status/2040744192020717592; 需要进行编码，因为有些东西使我的音频与视频不同步

[Responding to all the comments, so you may need to read through the thread again if I miss context here]

> The war isn't happening as long as you don't look at it.

I'm not ignorant of the war, and yes I follow it; however my mentality stems from the sheer number of months of bad news happening somewhere. I can only give so much of my attention to The World when there's things at the local level to worry about. So I don't engage in "all of it" on the Internet. I have those conversations in person.

> But the answer is not about drowning yourself in pleasant fiction on Amazon Prime or ignoring current events.

I disagree, but also not drowning. Rather engaging online in the manner pre-COVID. You know, like Walking Dead "How would I survive a zombie apocalypse" or Game of Thrones "who's next to die". The 24/7 Internet chatroom known as "the comments section" just wants to deviate the conversation back to politics. I also disagree with the sentiment that we're in "Idiocracy", but I still enjoy the film.

> The fast moving clickbait media of Twitter and Facebook is trash. It's often incorrect, it's full of propaganda

So is every aggregator site. Agenda-Setting Theory [1] dictates what information you're receiving on any given day, and smaller scale Discords with self-promotion know how to gamify Trending algorithms (since most rely on some degree of 'velocity' based on time since post). Couple that with how we've over engineered attention by A/B testing thumbnails [2] and how the mind reads TEXT LIKE THIS [3] (which was also A/B tested for email campaign clickthroughs), I'm left with a curiosity of "what headline text is emotionally anchoring a sentiment"?

Heck, GET OFF THE INTERNET isn't even yelling [4], it's just me abusing that all caps does thing in brain.

[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agenda-setting_theory

[2] https://netflixtechblog.com/selecting-the-best-artwork-for-v...

[3] https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10....

[4] Reddit Version: https://v.redd.it/hlo2z6a6rctg1, Twitter Version: https://x.com/amgaweda/status/2040744192020717592; Gotta work on encoding cause something made my audio out of sync with the video

cdrnsf 2026/04/07 00:06
我们还差得很远。这个国家正在由无能的阿谀奉承者统治，他们受制于一名正在社交媒体上犯下反人类罪的罪犯。他利用自己的私人准军事部队来恐吓任何他不喜欢的人，同时摧毁任何可能限制他的机构，努力颠覆投票，摧毁那些还不是非常富有的人的经济，加速破坏气候，破坏国际关系，破坏联盟。国会允许他而不是限制他，最高法院也是如此。
We are nowhere near fine. The country is being run by incompetent sycophants in thrall to a criminal who is musing about committing crimes against humanity on social media. He's using his own private paramilitary to terrorize anyone he dislikes all while gutting any institutions that may constrain him, working to subvert voting, destroying the economy for anyone that isn't already obscenely rich, destroying the climate at an accelerated rate, gutting international relations, destroying alliances. Congress enables him instead of checking him, as does the Supreme Court.

在 HN 中查看
sublinear 2026/04/06 23:41
事实上，“现实政治”几乎按照定义来说是： 不在线。

我认为很多人在特朗普的第一个任期内没有收到这份备忘录，并且在他的第二个任期内仍然对此感到困惑。

共和党人从来没有像民主党人那样利用媒体。保守价值观的变化非常缓慢，并通过军队、宗教等机构传播。特朗普将保守价值观提升到了一个新的水平，他只利用互联网来控制长期上网的人，并将其他人排除在外。这就是广播纪律。

The "realpolitik" is in fact, and almost by definition, not online.

I think a ton of people didn't get the memo during the first Trump term, and are still baffled by it during his second one.

Republicans have never used the media like the Democrats. Conservative values change very slowly and are disseminated through institutions like the military, religion, etc. Trump has taken it to the next level by only ever using the internet to troll the chronically online and anyone else out of the loop. That's radio discipline.

sublinear 2026/04/07 06:20
我以前也听过你说的话，我对这种说法同样感到困惑。

我并不是说共和党人对他们的计划保密。残酷的简单性是共和党选民的主要吸引力。他们强调 不想讨论。他们想要行动。没有什么可挑剔或分析的，这就是重点。与挥舞着大棒的人很难争辩。

这是引用自 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neoconservatism

> 卡内基国际事务伦理委员会高级研究员、新保守主义的著名批评家乔纳森·克拉克提出以下“新保守主义的主要特征”：“倾向于以善恶二元的方式看待世界”、“对外交的低容忍度”、“准备使用武力”、“强调美国的单边行动”、“蔑视多边组织”和“关注中东”。

自 20 世纪 70 年代以来，这都是同样的策略。如果你想听到任何关于它的辩论，你就必须追溯到那么久以前。今天的共和党中没有人会接受或重申这些，因为这只会让他们在选民面前显得软弱。

值得一看（乔姆斯基的《火线》剧集）： https://youtube.com/watch?v=9DvmLMUfGss

I've also heard what you're saying before and I'm equally confused by this take.

I'm not saying the Republicans keep their plans secret. The brutal simplicity is the main appeal for Republican voters. They emphatically don't want discussion. They want action. There's nothing to pick apart or analyze, and that's the point. It's hard to argue with someone waving a big stick.

Here's a quote from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neoconservatism

> Jonathan Clarke, a senior fellow at the Carnegie Council for Ethics in International Affairs and prominent critic of Neoconservatism, proposed the following as the "main characteristics of neoconservatism": "a tendency to see the world in binary good/evil terms", a "low tolerance for diplomacy", a "readiness to use military force", an "emphasis on US unilateral action", a "disdain for multilateral organizations" and a "focus on the Middle East".

This is the same game plan since the 1970s. If you want to hear any debate about it, you're gonna have to go that far back. Nobody in today's Republican party is ever going to entertain or reiterate any of this because it will just make them look weak to voters.

Worth a watch (the Chomsky episode of Firing Line): https://youtube.com/watch?v=9DvmLMUfGss

sublinear 2026/04/07 00:26
目前，新保守派正在领导共和党。他们也有一个非常清晰的游戏计划，不需要不断的喋喋不休。我只是说明价值观的起源，当然，如果没有强有力的领导，随着时间的推移，事情可能会变得模糊。

对于民主党来说，同样的问题是，当他们的选民基础越来越反社会并且不相信高等教育时，他们希望在哪里找到新的领导人。

bachmeier 2026/04/06 23:29
> 我们很好，关键是要记住离开互联网并记住现实与互联网不同。

这种方法效果很好，除非坏消息反映了现实，或者低估了现实的糟糕程度。在这种情况下，这就好像说癌症不是问题，问题是你去看了医生并听他告诉你坏消息。

> We're fine, the trick is to remember to GET OFF THE INTERNET and remember that reality isn't the same as the Internet.

That works fine, except in the cases where the bad news reflects reality, or understates how bad the reality is. In that case it's like saying cancer isn't the problem, the problem is that you visited the doctor and listened as he told you bad news.

在 HN 中查看
tsumnia 2026/04/07 07:05
> 这很好用，除非坏消息反映了现实

问题是 24/7 互联网聊天室/论坛每天都会改变“坏消息”目标。有时是战争，有时是自然灾害，有时是可怕的犯罪，等等。如果自新冠疫情以来你只看到坏消息，那么它会让你（读到，让我）认为世界正处于一个可怕的地方。我不再把所有时间都花在 24/7 聊天室里，当我在聊天室里说这句话时，每个人都认为我完全不知道。我不是。我只是忙于其他事情，比如当我的朋友发布一些东西时为他们加油。

> That works fine, except in the cases where the bad news reflects reality

The issue is that the 24/7 Internet chatroom/forums shift the "bad news" target on a daily basis. Sometimes its war, others its natural disaster, others its a horrific crime, etc. If you've been only seeing bad news since Covid, then it makes you (read, made me) think the world's in a terrible place. I stopped spending allll my time in the 24/7 chatroom and when I say this IN the chatroom everyone thinks I'm completely unaware. I'm not. I just engage on other matters, like cheering on my buddies when they release something.

在 HN 中查看
dragonwriter 2026/04/07 07:41
世界（美国也是）比几年前更加可怕，很大一部分原因是， 他们是否保持在线，人们无助地脱离事件；幸福地无知对社会的影响与因真实、错误和虚假信息过度饱和而陷入瘫痪状态没有本质上的不同，即使它在短期内更令人愉快。

关闭源（尤其是那些被更加极端地操纵以产生无效愤怒的源，这是 世界变得更糟的一部分）可能是管理当前媒体和物质条件组合的短期压力的有效方法，但它对实际解决物质条件没有任何作用。哎呀，脱离和复员以减少对任意行使权力的抵抗是你被操纵的一个重要部分 为了。这并非偶然，它能起到缓解压力的作用。这是操纵设计的一部分。

The world is (and the US is) a measurably more terrible place than only a few years ago, and a big part of the reason is that, whether or not they remain online, people are helplessly detached from events; being blissfully ignorant is not substantively different in societal impact than being in a state of paralysis from oversaturation of a mix of real, mis- and dis-informaton, even if it is more enjoyable in the near term.

Shutting off the feeds (especially those that are becoming more extremely manipulated to produce ineffective rage, which is part of how the world is worse) may be an effective way to manage the near-term stress of the present combination of media and material conditions, but it doesn't do anything to actually address the material conditions. Heck, detachment and demobilization to reduce resistance to arbitrary exercise of power is a big part of what you are being manipulated for. It's not an accident that that works as stress relief; that's part of the design of the manipulation.

在 HN 中查看
tsumnia 2026/04/07 08:39
> 世界（和美国）比几年前更加可怕

我既不同意也不不同意（如果这有道理的话），但我当然同意现代互联网已经扭曲了人们对事物的看法。我通过 Spotify BetterHelp 广告听说它被称为“屏幕排毒”，虽然我从未使用过该服务，但我明白他们的意思。

早在去年 Digg 4.0 期间，一位核心用户就将其称为“在参加骚乱时试图进行对话”。很多第三方和不知名的用户名都在插话，如果你不回答所有这些，印象可能会同样扭曲对话的初衷。甚至在最初的评论之后对话如何进行也很有趣。

我只是认为新冠疫情让我们所有人“志同道合”，然后有人通过 Reddit 的严格审核等方式玷污了这一点。就像，我们都在做自己的小事，然后“每个人”都在刷新约翰霍普金斯大学的仪表板，想知道他们是否因为苏伊士运河而有足够的卫生纸，或者观看其他州发生的所有抗议/骚乱。

但失去的是没有人出去做事，把钱存起来/赌在下一次轧空上，并且不支持当地的东西。如果有的话，“离开互联网”是我在操纵/心理战/营销活动方面的尝试。而且，在本地，是的，我们离线，公开谈论我们在不同平台上看到的内容，因为 Reddit 和 Twitter 具有政治倾向，并且有点记得大流行之前的一段时间。

我现在去 LGS 参加万智牌活动。在那个“缺失的第三空间”里见到书呆子真是太酷了。我们仍在谈论关税和全球冲突。我们只是尊重地这样做，而不是试图同时破坏游戏，因为并不是每个人都同意。我什至可以分辨出某人何时刚离开竞技场，因为他们玩了一些即时获胜的元组合。我只是制作部落套牌，我没有时间研究所有这些。

> The world is (and the US is) a measurably more terrible place than only a few years ago

I neither agree nor disagree (if that makes sense), but I certainly agree that being modern Internet has warped people's views on things. I hear it called a "screen detox" via my Spotify BetterHelp ads and while I never used that service, I get what they mean.

Back during Digg 4.0 last year, one of the core members of users referred to it as "trying to have a conversation while attending a riot". Its a lot of third parties and faceless usernames chiming in, and if you don't answer all of them the impression can get equally get warped about the original intent of the conversation. Even how the conversation gets steered after the original comment is interesting to see.

I just think Covid made us all "get on the same wavelength", then someone(s) tainted that through things like heavy Reddit moderation. Like, we were all doing our own little things, then "everyone" is refreshing Johns Hopkins' dashboard, wondering if they have enough toilet paper because of the Seuz Canal, or watching all of the protest/riots unfold in other states.

But what got lost was no one going out to things, saving/gambling their money on the next short squeeze, and not supporting local stuff. If anything, GET OFF THE INTERNET is my attempt at manipulation/psyop/marketing campaign. And, locally, yeah, we're offline, openly talking about what we see on the different platforms since Reddit and Twitter are politically skewed, and sort of remembering a time before the pandemic.

I go to Magic the Gathering events at my LGS now. Its pretty cool to meet the nerds in that "missing third space". We're still talking about tariffs and global conflicts. We're just doing it respectfully and not trying to ruin the game at the same time cause not everyone agrees. I can even tell when someone is fresh off Arena because they play some of those insta-win meta combos. I just make tribal decks, I don't have time to study all that.

bachmeier 2026/04/07 03:27
是的，这就是贝佐斯写的。我严重怀疑它以意见部分结束：

> 我写这封信是为了让您了解我们的意见页面即将发生的变化……我们将每天撰写文章以支持捍卫两大支柱：个人自由和自由市场。当然，我们也会讨论其他主题，但反对这些支柱的观点将由其他人发表......

> I’m writing to let you know about a change coming to our opinion pages...We are going to be writing every day in support of defense of two pillars: personal liberties and free markets. I'll leave it to others to make a decision on whether WAPO qualifies as a propaganda outfit.

pixl97 2026/04/07 01:20
虽然这些列出的文件可能不是彻头彻尾的捏造，但它们在很大程度上受到了亿万富翁所有者想让你知道的信息的操纵。

这里的部分问题是，你只能列出几篇可能告诉你真相的论文，而在过去，有更多的独立新闻机构会相互竞争。现在他们需要先与股东核实。

While those listed papers may not be outright fabrications, they are very much manipulated by what their billionaire owners want you to know.

Part of the problem here is you can only list a few papers that might tell you the truth at all, when in the past there was far more independent news organizations that would vie against each other. Now they need to check in with their shareholders first.

suzzer99 2026/04/07 01:42
《华盛顿邮报》的每位记者和编辑在报道付印之前都不会与贝索斯核实。是的，所有者操纵了一些东西并删除了一些文章，但在大多数情况下，美国主要报纸上仍然有非常好的报道。由于亿万富翁所有者正在蚕食边缘，因此放弃所有已建立的新闻业是一个方便的谬论。
Every WaPo reporter and editor doesn't check in with Bezos before a story goes to print. Yeah, the owners steer some stuff and kill some articles, but for the most part there's still very good reporting going on at the major US papers. It's a convenient fallacy to handwave away all established journalism because billionaire owners are chipping away around the edges.

pixl97 2026/04/07 02:49
>《华盛顿邮报》记者和编辑在报道付印之前不会与贝佐斯联系。

记者在名单的最底层，有一堆中高层管理人员为贝索斯做所有这些工作，而不需要他去关注。

只需要他打来一个电话，说他们需要在某个话题上小心谨慎，仅此而已。调查该主题的资金枯竭。

Now, I never said 'throw away' journalism, I said to ensure you understand the bias of the paper in question. Just because WaPo isn't reporting on Bezos doesn't mean there isn't anything to report on said guy.

>WaPo reporter and editor doesn't check in with Bezos before a story goes to print.

Reporters are at the bottom of the list, there is a pile of middle and upper management that does all this work for Bezos without his need to keep an eye on it.

All it takes is one phone call from him saying they need to be careful around a topic and that's it. Funds dry up for investigations into that topic.

Now, I never said 'throw away' journalism, I said to ensure you understand the bias of the paper in question. Just because WaPo isn't reporting on Bezos doesn't mean there isn't anything to report on said guy.

mPogrzeb 2026/04/06 23:26

在 HN 中查看
vharuck 2026/04/07 00:39
>我们很好，关键是要记住离开互联网并记住现实与互联网不同。

我可以理解为什么有人能够保持这种安慰：它曾经（大部分）是真实的。政治“丑闻”通常要么确实很糟糕，但却是局部性的（例如，政客因贿赂而被捕并被开除），或者是表演性的愤怒（例如，徽章）。

现在不同了。那个时代是我们的“职业摔跤”时代：认真的专业人士投入工作，但也通过表演来留住粉丝。不管剧本有多脏，每个人都确保灯一直亮着。现在我们正处于“青少年街头帮派”时代。 “表演”实际上是他们看待世界的方式，参与者实际上以身体疼痛为乐，而旁观的公民只是感到恐惧。

我无法理解，在儿童疫苗小组尝试做的事情之后，怎么会有人认为事情会好起来。或者诺姆扣留救济资金。或者是一揽子关税，而没有任何进一步的计划来改善我们的产业。这些行为对民众产生了巨大的负面影响。绝大多数公民 有 这些选择造成了不必要的伤害。

>We're fine, the trick is to remember to GET OFF THE INTERNET and remember that reality isn't the same as the Internet.

I can understand how somebody could hold onto this comfort: it used to be (mostly) true. Political "scandals" were usually either truly bad but localized (e.g. a politician caught and kicked out for bribery) or performative furor (e.g. a lapel pin).

It's different now. Those times were our "pro wrestling" era: earnest professionals who put in the work but also put on a show to keep the fans. No matter how dirty the script got, everyone made sure the lights stayed on. Now we're in the "teenage street gang" era. The "show" is actually how they see the world, participants literally delight in physical pain, and citizens on the sidelines are only terrorized.

How anyone could think things would be fine after what the childhood vaccine panel tried to do is beyond me. Or Noem withholding relief funds. Or blanket tariffs without any further plan for improving our industries. Those acts have huge negative effects across the population. The vast majority of citizens have been needlessly harmed by those choices.

在 HN 中查看
tsumnia 2026/04/07 07:17
> 那个时代是我们的“职业摔跤”时代

我不同意，但遗憾的是我认为文字无法提供我完整的推理。但这源于“现代WWE”（AEW现在与布罗迪·金相比是一种不同的“文化”），但将其与奥勒·安德森[1]被粉丝刺伤相比，而如今大多数摔跤手都被人们蜂拥而至，希望他们在易趣转售品上签名。再说一次，更多的是关于粉丝（不是 IWC）如何对待现实生活中的摔跤手，而不是政治。 （除此之外，看起来这也是一次试图刺伤的行为，所以可能是一个有争议的问题 [2]）

我认为政治现在刚刚进入“职业摔跤”时代。是的，这很大程度上要归功于过去出现在 WWE 上的某位总统。

[1] https://www.midatlanticwrestling.net/resourcecenter/gateway_...

[2] https://www.fightful.com/wrestling/krule-says-he-is-fine-aft...

> Those times were our "pro wrestling" era

I disagree, but sadly I don't think text will provide my full reasoning. But it stems from "modern WWE" (AEW is a different 'culture' right now with Brodie King), but comparing it to Ole Anderson [1] getting stabbed by a fan compared to these day when most wrestlers are just getting swarmed by people wanting them to autograph their ebay resells. Again, more about how fans (not IWC) treat wrestlers IRL, not politics. (Aside, looks like the was an attempted stabbing too so may be a moot point [2])

I think politics has just now entered its "pro wrestling" era. And yes, its largely due to a certain President that's appeared on WWE in the past.

[1] https://www.midatlanticwrestling.net/resourcecenter/gateway_...

[2] https://www.fightful.com/wrestling/krule-says-he-is-fine-aft...

luisln 2026/04/06 22:50
这在美国以外可能是可能的，但在美国互联网已经成为现实。 特朗普发推文会影响金融市场。人们在 X 上发帖，迅速走红，并被 OpenAI 聘用。过滤掉有关机构不稳定的新闻并不会让机构变得更加稳定，只会让你更少了解它。也许有一天你会发现自己实际上面临着这种后果，却不知道如何才能避免它。
This might be possible outside the US, but in the US the internet has become reality. Trump tweets and it effects financial markets. People post on X, go viral, get hired by OpenAI. Filtering out news about institutional instability doesn't make institutions more stable, it just makes you less informed about it. And maybe one day you'll find yourself actually facing the consequences of that without knowing how you could have prevented it.

极度在线和把头埋在沙子里有明显的区别。美国也不好啊美国在海外发动非法侵略战争，犯下战争罪行，并发出更多战争罪行。美国入侵了自己的城市，包括我的城市，未经训练的暴徒射杀了多名美国公民。

如果您不知道正在发生的事情，这将如何影响您的政治观点？你的开支？你的习惯？你的投票？

编辑 - 还有一些：

- 伊朗战争正在引发全球能源和经济危机。结果，全球燃料价格飞涨，一些国家规定人们每周工作时间不得超过几天（因此无法获得报酬）以节省燃料。这推高了杂货、材料和其他商品的价格，这将对全球穷人造成不成比例的影响。许多人将无法生存。

- 美国一直故意非法禁止向古巴运送石油和天然气，导致该国陷入停电和不稳定，这也违反了国际法。人们无法工作、做饭、冷藏食物或开灯。

你确定我们没事吗？

编辑2：已经投反对票了！惊人的。很高兴看到硅谷的右翼倾向依然活跃。期待着市场崩溃的那一天，你所有的 RSU 和股票都值得他妈的了。你不能吃股票，但你可以吃富人。

There's a stark difference between being Extremely Online and sticking your head in the sand. The US is not fine. The US is waging an illegal war of aggression abroad, committing war crimes and threatening more. The US has invaded its own cities, mine included, with untrained goons who have shot and killed multiple US citizens.

If you're not aware of what's happening, how will that impact your political views? Your spending? Your habits? Your vote?

Edit - A few more:

- The war in Iran is triggering an energy and economic crisis globally. Fuel prices are skyrocketing globally as a result, with some countries mandating that people cannot work (thus, cannot get paid) more than a few days a week to preserve fuel. This is pushing up prices on groceries, materials and other goods that will disproportionately impact the global poor. Many will not be able to survive.

- The US has been intentionally and illegally embargoing oil and gas shipments to Cuba plunging the country into blackouts and instability, also against international law. People can't work, or cook, or refrigerate food, or turn on their lights.

You sure we're fine?

Edit 2: Downvotes already! Amazing. Good to see the right wing slant in Silicon Valley is alive and well. Looking forward to the day the market crashes and all of your RSUs and stock holdings are worth fuck all. You can't eat stocks, but you can eat the rich.

"reality isn't the same as the internet" was already starting to be a dangerously out-of-touch delusion when Boomers and Silents were saying it in the 1990s.

dragonwriter 2026/04/07 07:26
> 从外部观察美国，我开始怀疑他们离社会崩溃有多近。

美国并不是特别接近（至少不太可能）社会崩溃；从某种意义上说，这是一种过于乐观的立场。政府、秩序和结构化社会并未面临崩溃的迫在眉睫的危险。

它 是 非常接近从自由民主政府转向支持非常不同的东西的转变。 [0]

[0] 可以说， 过去的 到了这一点，但已经接近了人们广泛接受的程度，即这不是一种暂时的失常，基本的文化和制度支持仍然完好无损，并且能够使事情恢复原样。

> Looking at the US from outside, I am starting to wonder how close they are to a societal collapse.

The US is not particularly close (at least, not highly probable) to a societal collapse; that's, in a sense, an overly optimistic position. Government, order, and structured society are not in imminent danger of collapse.

It is very close to a transition away from liberal democratic government in favor of something very different. [0]

[0] Arguably, past that point, but close to the point where it becomes widely accepted that the it wasn't a temporary aberration where the basic cultural and institutional supports were still intact and capable of snapping things back.

sandy_coyote 2026/04/07 00:39
我也觉得这些内容令人反感，但它有点符合美国的历史，因为美国是一个主要由傲慢的流氓统治的国家，他们对国内外的穷人感到反感。既然企业“新闻”媒体和社交媒体算法已经找到了通过鼓励仇恨来获利的合法途径，他们只是不再试图隐藏它。
I also find the content distasteful, but it kinda tracks with US history as a country run mostly by cavalier bruisers with antipathy to the have-nots both domestic and abroad. zeroonetwothree 2026/04/07 00:31
美国政府没有良好的记录。我觉得任何认为情况特别糟糕的人现在都需要读一些历史书。显然，我希望情况能更好，但正是这个组织在 20 世纪给你带来了十几场战争、日本拘留、强制隔离、价格管制、对平民使用核武器等等。

我的猜测是，这更多地与阅读特别符合一种政治观点的新闻来源有关，而不是政府正在做的事情的实际情况。

US government does not have a good record. I feel like anyone that thinks it’s particularly bad now needs to read some history books. Obviously I wish it were better but this is the same group that brought you a dozen wars in the 20th century, Japanese internment, forced segregation, price controls, nuclear weapons used on civilians, and so on.

My guess is that it has more to do with reading news sources particularly aligned with one political viewpoint than the actual facts of what the government is doing.

在 HN 中查看
keiferski 2026/04/07 00:38
这种观点只有在不看历史的情况下才显得合乎逻辑。我很难想象一个今天有效但在不久的过去没有采取一些可怕行动的政府。我认为充其量，你会得到像瑞士或丹麦这样功能上“次要”的国家，这些国家并没有真正处于美国曾经/现在所处的强大地位。

因此，最好将美国政府的记录与其他州的记录进行比较，在这种比较中，我认为美国的表现相当不错。不是最好的，但肯定不是最差的。

This kind of opinion seems logical only if you don't look at history. I'm struggling to think of a government which is effective today but didn't have some horrible actions in the near past. At best I think you'll get functionally "minor" states like Switzerland or Denmark that weren't really in the powerful position the US was/is in.

And so it's much better to compare the US government's record with the record of other states, and in that comparison I think the US comes out reasonably well. Not the best, but certainly not the worst.

palmotea 2026/04/07 01:49
> 好消息是，特朗普政权不受欢迎，而疯狂的事情却让他们更不受欢迎。

事实上，这也是个坏消息。正是这种应对措施说服民主党人把头埋在沙子里，避免与其他人打交道。 他们的 问题，这为特朗普创造了机会。他们更关心自己的正统观念，而不是真正成为一个受欢迎的政党，赢得真正的多数并结束这种胡言乱语。

因此，我们目前的方针是：民主党仍然不受欢迎，并希望在 2026 年中期选举中获胜，可能会做一些他们自己的疯狂事情，然后在 2028 年我们可能会出现新的 MAGA 疯狂人士。

崩溃实际上是两党共同的，每个主要政党都有不同的功能障碍。

> The good news is that the Trump regime is unpopular, and doing crazy things is making them more unpopular.

Actually, that's bad news too. It's the cope that's convincing Democrats to stick their head in the sand and avoid dealing with their problems, which are what created the opening for Trump. They're more concerned with their own orthodoxies than actually becoming a popular party that could win a real majority and end this nonsense.

So our present course is: Democrats remain unpopular and eek out a win in the midterms in 2026, probably do some nutty things of their own, and then in 2028 we'll likely get new MAGA nutjobs.

The collapse is actually bipartisan, with different dysfunctions in each major party.

kristopolous 2026/04/07 00:28
离它不远。有些部分我不喜欢，但宅基地、奴隶制、排华、红线、日本人拘留、三K党和吉姆·克劳都不太棒。

这就是美国的行为：粗鲁、残忍、敌对、傲慢、傲慢无知。

理查德·霍夫施塔特 (Richard Hofstadter) 曾描述过 20 世纪 60 年代美国人的这种做法。

看看 1924 年颁布的《约翰逊-里德法案》，该法案已经持续了数十年。那些感情并没有消失……

而且也没有“好州”——1879年的加州宪法设立了针对华人的种族隔离制度。甚至还有第二个叫做“中国人”。

俄勒冈州以“仅限白人”条款明确加入联邦。

《独立宣言》甚至还有关于“无情的印度野蛮人”的疯狂阴谋论

再多的经验证据也无法让美国人意识到这一点，因为这让他们皱起了眉头。

所以无论如何都没有。一切如常

Nowhere near it. There's parts I don't like but it's not like Homesteading, slavery, Chinese exclusion, redlining, Japanese internment, the klan, and Jim Crow were great.

This is American behavior: crude, cruel, hostile, arrogant, and proudly ignorant.

Richard Hofstadter wrote about Americans acting this way in the 1960s.

Look at the Johnson-Reed Act of 1924, stood for decades. It's not like those sentiments went away...

And there's no "good states" either - the California Constitution in 1879 set up a racial apartheid system against Chinese people. Even had a second called "The Chinese".

Oregon was admitted to the Union explicitly with a "whites only" clause.

The Declaration of Independence even has wild conspiracy theories about "merciless Indian savages"

No amount of empirical evidence will make Americans realize this because it gives them a frowny face.

So anyways no. This is all business as usual

kristopolous 2026/04/07 04:00
关键是有一种关于美国好与坏的二分法的错误叙述，人们喜欢声称自己来自好部分......

历史并没有真正揭露这一点，美国的偷窃者偏见似乎只是重新定位而不是消退。

例如，最大规模的大规模私刑是针对意大利人的。

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1891_New_Orleans_lynchings

反爱尔兰骚乱？当然费城怎么样： https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philadelphia_nativist_riots

这段历史在美国随处可见。

或者看看 20 世纪 70 年代的布朗克斯或 1921 年的塔尔萨。我们不需要敌人向我们的城市投下炸弹 - 我们自己做到了

所以当人们说“这个美国社会正在崩溃吗？”

我说“不。这就是美国社会”

The point is there's this false narrative about a dichotomy of bad and good America where people like to claim they're from the good part...

The history doesn't really bare that out and the peculator American prejudice seems to simply retarget more often than recede.

The largest mass lynching, for instance, was against Italians.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1891_New_Orleans_lynchings

Anti Irish riots? Sure what about Philadelphia : https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philadelphia_nativist_riots

This history is everywhere in the USA.

Or look at the Bronx in the 1970s or Tulsa in 1921. We didn't need bombs to drop on our cities from an adversary - we did it to ourselves

So when people say "is this American society breaking down?"

I say "no. This is simply American society"

在 HN 中查看
dweez 2026/04/07 00:45
现代世界就像让·鲍德里亚（Jean Baudrillard）笔下的地狱。早在 1991 年，他就写了《海湾战争没有发生》，评论了当时媒体对战争的 24/7 现场直播报道的新发展。媒体饱和创造了一种超现实，关于战争的图像取代了事物本身。我们已经走了多远。我们在这里如此自满，以至于战争仅以符号和声音从我们的屏幕中流出而存在。我认为很多人并不真正相信这是真的。
The modern world is like Jean Baudrillard's vision of hell. Back in 1991 he wrote "The Gulf War did not take place", commenting on what was at the time a new development of 24/7 live media coverage of the war. Media saturation created a hyperreality where images about the war replaced the thing itself. How far we have come. We are so complacent here that war exists only as stream of symbols and sounds streaming out of our screen. I think many do not truly believe it is real.
在 HN 中查看
dangus 2026/04/06 22:44
我不知道我是否会称之为人工智能宣传的新时代，就像我称之为“不严肃、不专业、不合格的独裁领导人宁愿欺骗他们的支持基础，也不愿为社会问题提供严肃的政策解决方案”一样。

我们可以在这篇文章中注意到，房间里明显没有使用这些策略的成熟成年人。我们没有看到大量政党资助的人工智能模因试图推销全民医疗保健、加强公共服务和教育、消除贫困和无家可归、解决生活成本危机、结束对汽油的依赖等。

现在是人工智能为没有好创意的人宣传的时代，因为人工智能是好创意的替代品。

I don't know if I would call this the new age of AI propaganda as much as I would call this "unserious, unprofessional, unqualified, authoritarian leaders would rather deceive their support base than offer serious policy solutions to societal problems."

We can notice in this article the conspicuous absence of the mature adults in the room using these tactics. We don't see a whole lot of party-sponsored AI memes trying to sell universal healthcare, enhanced public services and education, ending poverty and homelessness, addressing cost of living crisis, ending gasoline dependency, etc.

It's the age of AI propaganda for people with no good ideas, because AI is a substitute for good ideas.

palmotea 2026/04/07 01:59
> 也许人工智能内容的数量最终会让人们提倡媒体意识培训，而不是像目前那样试图责骂平台只显示他们所支持的操纵性内容。

不会发生的。最好的结果是，人们对人工智能内容的泛滥感到恐惧，产生一种本能的冲动，这超越了正常的道德考虑，导致暴民烧毁一些数据中心（或者支持那些同样鲁莽的政客）。

不会发生的是，大多数人脑子已经烂了十年，然后因为接受了几个小时的“媒体意识培训”而进行了某种改革。

> Maybe the volume of AI content will finally get people to advocate for media awareness training, rather than the current strat of trying to scold platforms into only showing the manipulative content they side with.

Not gonna happen. The best outcome is there's an organic impulse to recoil in horror to the flood of AI content, which overrides normal moral considerations and causes mobs to go burn down some datacenters (or support politicians who'd do something equally brash).

What's not going to happen is the majority gets brain-rotted for a decade and then somehow reforms because they had a couple hours of "media awareness training."

AndrewKemendo 2026/04/06 22:40
> 无论其来源或意图如何，最引人注目的内容都会赢得最大的影响力。

“获胜”意味着你成功地操纵了一个推理能力极差的人，他们会根据宣传做出反应并做出决定

如果多数人没有能力或愿望积极抵制操纵，那么他们就生活在他们满意的世界中

> the most compelling content wins the most reach regardless of its origin or intent.

在 HN 中查看
Eextra953 2026/04/07 00:06
宣传对具有各种“推理能力”的人都起作用。没有人能幸免。此外，良好宣传的一个特点是，它可以通过人们的废话过滤器，使他们甚至不知道自己正在被操纵。文章指出，当前使用乐高宣传作为政府更新工具的例子，以便将信息传达给更多人。

这很重要，因为它可以让那些不知道自己被操纵的人建立起多元化。在民主制度中，多数可以产生多数，而多数可以创造权力。所有这些都是为了说明：我不认为那些被宣传所迷惑的人生活在一个他们满意的世界中，相反，他们生活在一个他们被告知他们满意的世界中，而缺乏相反的叙述并没有向他们展示更好的方式。考虑到当某些东西不自然受欢迎或对大多数人不利时，宣传就会被淘汰，这就是为什么我们看到宣传最常用于军事行动。

Propaganda works on people with all levels of 'capacity for reasoning'. No one is immune to it. Also, a feature of good propaganda is that it gets through a persons bullshit filter so that they are not even aware that they are being manipulated. The article points out the current use of Lego propaganda as examples of governments updating their tools so that they get their message across to more people.

This is important because it lets pluralities build from people who are not aware they are being manipulated. Pluralities can lead to majorities and majorities, in a democratic system, create power. All this to say: I don't think those who have fallen for propaganda are living in a world they are satisfied with but instead that they are living in a world they've been told they are satisfied with and a lack of counter narratives have not shown them a better way. Consider that propaganda gets busted out whenever something isn't naturally popular or beneficial to most people, that is why we see propaganda most used around military efforts.

AndrewKemendo 2026/04/07 00:20
如果某人继续将自己置身于宣传过多的情况和环境中，那么这表明他们缺乏核心的洞察力

人们在受到宣传的影响下无法拥有代理权的想法完全是荒谬的，而且显然是不正确的

有数以百万计的人可以辨别宣传并根据真实数据做出决策

If someone is continuing to put themselves into situations and contexts where they are overloaded with propaganda, then that indicates they lack a core level of discernment

The idea that people cannot have agency while being subjected to propaganda is totally fucking absurd and demonstrably not true

there are millions of examples of people who can discern propaganda and make decisions based on ground truth data

pixl97 2026/04/07 01:33
>将自己置于宣传超载的情况和环境中，

啊，是的，我们大多数人都是独立富裕的，并且拥有财务自由，可以避免整天在大堂里播放福克斯新闻。

是的，如果人们愿意的话，他们可以拥有代理权，但大多数人选择不这样做，因为这样做需要大量的精力。但你从第一原则出发，凭一己之力创造了社会，对吗？哦，你没有。

问题是，尤其是在媒体无处不在的现代世界，我们所做的几乎所有事情都受到宣传的影响。新闻、广告、生活方式，无一不充满着宣传。其中一些对您来说是显而易见的，您可以很快地对其打折扣。它的其他部分是伴随你成长的东西，你甚至没有第一印象知道它是宣传。

简而言之，人类脆弱的思维无法理解或感知所有现实的基本事实，因此我们会受到从他人那里收到的带有偏见和过滤的信息的影响。

>put themselves into situations and contexts where they are overloaded with propaganda,

Ah, yes, most of us are independently wealthy and have the financial freedom to avoid working in lobbies blasting fox news all day.

Yes, people can have agency if they choose to, most choose not to because it takes a massive fuckton of energy to do so. But you created society all by yourself from first principles right? Oh you didn't.

The thing is, especially in the modern world with so much media everywhere all the time, we are being subjected to propaganda in nearly everything we do. News, advertizing, lifestyle, all are imbued with propaganda. Some of it's obvious to you and you can quickly discount it. Other parts of it are something you've grown up with and you don't even have the first clue that it is propaganda.

Simply put your feeble human mind cannot comprehend nor perceive the ground truth of all reality and therefore we're subject to the biased and filtered information we receive from others.

rexpop 2026/04/07 05:52
> 人们创造自己的历史，但他们并不是随心所欲地创造历史；他们不是在自我选择的环境下成功的，而是在已经存在的、给定的和从过去传承下来的环境下实现的。所有死去的世代的传统就像一场噩梦压在生者的脑海中。

——卡尔·马克思，《路易·波拿巴的雾月十八日》

> Men make their own history, but they do not make it as they please; they do not make it under self-selected circumstances, but under circumstances existing already, given and transmitted from the past. The tradition of all dead generations weighs like a nightmare on the brains of the living.

— Karl Marx, The Eighteenth Brumaire of Louis Bonaparte

don_esteban 2026/04/07 02:01
确实，这样的人还有很多。他们仍然是极少数。

我认识的几乎每个人都倾向于不过度检查与他们的世界观大体一致的陈述，并对相反的陈述不屑一顾（作为宣传，而不做检查真实数据的工作）。

For sure, there are plenty of such people. They are still very small minority.

Almost everybody I know has a tendency to not overly check the statements that broadly align with their world view, and be dismissive (as propaganda, without doing the work to check the ground truth data) of statements that are contrary.

在 HN 中查看
franklinter 2026/04/07 00:30
宣传对聪明人也很有效。它通常是这样运作的：聪明人喜欢消息灵通。他们源源不断地喝着涵盖广泛主题的“新闻”。因此，他们对时事的了解广泛但肤浅。

通常为他们选择主题（编辑器或算法）。新闻被打包供他们一目了然地形成观点（标题/社交媒体帖子）。他们缺乏深刻的理解，因此大多数事情都显得可信，尽管有时有些牵强。他们深入了解的主题几乎总是“覆盖率很低”，但他们不深入了解的主题则不然。

Propaganda works very well on smart people too. Here's how it often works: smart people like to be well informed. They drink a steady drip of "news" covering a wide range of topics. They therefore have a wide but shallow understanding of current events.

The topics are usually chosen for them (editor or algorithm). The news is packaged up for them to form an opinion at a glance (headline/social media post). They lack a deep understanding and so most things pass as largely believable, if at times a bit of a stretch. Topics they know deeply are almost always "covered poorly," but not the topics they don't know deeply.

在 HN 中查看
AndrewKemendo 2026/04/07 00:45
你所做的只是描述了这样一个事实：表面上聪明的人实际上并不聪明

我非常了解很多不受宣传的人 - 你不会认为他们特别“有趣”，因为他们通常专注于他们真正相信的事情，比如成为一名和尚

我认识的僧人几乎为零，特别是在西藏传统中，他们不能立即进行宣传——你可以提出一个强有力的论点，即佛教本身就是宣传，我实际上在广义上同意你的观点，但从某种意义上说，我们所描述的是一个你发现自己不知所措的背景。

哦，如果你真的那么聪明，那么你会读到下面的引言：

“如果你不读报纸，你就无知。如果你读了，你就会被误导。”

如果你包括那些无法区分可测量的可重复数据和宣传的人，你对“聪明人”的标准可能会比应有的水平低得多

https://marktwainstudies.com/the-apocryphal-twain/if-you-don...

All you did was describe the fact that people who present as intelligent aren’t actually intelligent

I’m very well aware of a lot of people who are not subject to propaganda - you wouldn’t consider them particularly “fun “to be around because usually they are dedicated and focused on something that they actually believe in like being a monk

I know literally zero monks especially in the tibetan tradition that cannot clock propaganda immediately - you could make a strong argument that Buddhism itself is propaganda and I would actually largely concur with you there in the broad sense however in the sense that we’re describing it is a context that you find yourself overwhelmed by

Oh and then if you actually are that smart then you would have read the following quote:

“ If you don’t read the newspaper, you’re uninformed. If you do, you’re misinformed.”

Your bar for “smart people” is probably way lower than it should be if you include people who can’t discriminate between measurable repeatible data and propaganda

https://marktwainstudies.com/the-apocryphal-twain/if-you-don...

在 HN 中查看
bigbadfeline 2026/04/07 04:12
> 如果你包括那些无法区分可测量的可重复数据和宣传的人，那就应该是这样

您严重低估了查找和处理“可测量的可重复数据”所涉及的成本和精力。事实上，对于大多数具有重要政治意义的问题来说，这样的问题并不存在，无论是由于不可能或重复性不足，还是无法进入少数可能有这种问题的地方。

绝大多数聪明人无法在不危及自身生存的情况下访问或处理此类数据。

此外，历史的重复性是一个有争议的问题，尽管闹剧很常见，但历史并没有真正重复。

> 我认识的僧人几乎为零，尤其是在西藏传统中，不能立即进行宣传

这只能表明你不太了解宣传是多么普遍，并且你接受了其中的很多内容作为事实。

> 你可以强有力地论证佛教本身就是宣传

好的，所以他们不会“立即进行宣传”。而且整个佛教不是宣传，世界很复杂 与人类个体的思维能力相比漫长而艰辛的科学史应该告诉你这一点。

下面的“stevenwoo”写了一些您可能会受益的内容：

“雅克·埃鲁尔（Jacques Ellul）的《宣传》一书中的事后分析提出了几个令人信服的论点，即宣传无处不在，并且对于大多数公众来说很难反击和 对自己的判断失去信心往往是致命弱点 这使得宣传能够潜入一个人的大脑。”

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47663236

> it should be if you include people who can’t discriminate between measurable repeatible data and propaganda

You severely underestimate the cost and effort involved in finding and processing "measurable repeatable data". In fact, for most politically important issues such don't exist, be it for reasons of impossible or insufficient repeatability or lack of access to the few places that may have it.

The vast majority of smart people cannot have access to, or process such data, in amount of time that doesn't risk their existence.

Besides, historical repeatability is kind of a moot point, history doesn't really repeat although farces are common.

> I know literally zero monks especially in the tibetan tradition that cannot clock propaganda immediately

That only shows that you don't quite have an idea how ubiquitous propaganda is, and you accept a lot of it as truth.

> you could make a strong argument that Buddhism itself is propaganda

OK, so they don't "clock propaganda immediately". Moreover, the whole of Buddhism isn't propaganda, the world is quite complicated compared to the abilities of the individual human mind, the long and arduous history of science should tell you that much.

'stevenwoo' below wrote something you might benefit from:

"the post hoc analysis in Jacques Ellul’s book Propaganda makes several compelling arguments that propaganda is omnipresent and difficult for the great part of public to counter and misplaced confidence in one’s own judgment is often the Achilles heel that allows propaganda to insinuate itself in one’s brain."

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47663236

作为一个普遍概念，人们更加持怀疑态度。

There’s an equilibrium point that is available

One that is more skeptical as a general concept.

However this comes at a significant cost most people choose not to bear

stevenwoo 2026/04/07 00:37
对 1960 年趋势的分析导致人们相信宗教和种族主义分歧会减少，这是可笑的，但雅克·埃鲁尔 (Jacques Ellul) 的《宣传》一书中的事后分析提出了几个令人信服的论点，即宣传无处不在，对大多数公众来说很难反击，而对自己判断的错误信心往往是让宣传在一个人的大脑中潜移默化的致命弱点。我今天在另一篇评论中提到了这本书，但它具有惊人的相关性。
The analysis of 1960 trends leading to belief that religion and racism divides would diminish is laughable but the post hoc analysis in Jacques Ellul’s book Propaganda makes several compelling arguments that propaganda is omnipresent and difficult for the great part of public to counter and misplaced confidence in one’s own judgment is often the Achilles heel that allows propaganda to insinuate itself in one’s brain. I mentioned this book in another comment today but it’s uncannily relevant.
